| **pollster** | 0.3.0 | 0.4.0 | Low | Async block_on |
| **toml** | 0.8.23 | 1.1.2 | Low | Config parsing |

## wgpu 23 → 29 (High effort)

**v24**: All wgpu objects now `Clone` (internally refcounted) — drop `Arc` wrappers. `Instance::new()` takes `&InstanceDescriptor`.
**v25**: `device.poll()` API reworked (`Maintain` → `PollType`). Vulkan/GLES backends now behind feature flags.
**v26**: `as_hal` calls return guards instead of callbacks. DX12 no longer needs `dxil.dll`.
**v27**: `RenderPassDescriptor` gains `multiview_mask`. `enumerate_adapters` now async. `MipmapFilterMode` split from `FilterMode`.
**v28**: Mesh shaders. Deferred command encoding (perf change). Stricter validation.
**v29**: `Surface::get_current_texture` returns new enum (affects render loop). `InstanceDescriptor` loses `Default`, must use `new_with_display_handle()`. `WriteOnly<[u8]>` replaces `&mut [u8]` for mapped buffers. `DepthStencilState` fields become `Option`. MSRV: 1.87.

## cosmic-text 0.12 → 0.18 (Medium effort, best ROI)

**v0.13**: `ShapeBuffer` moved into `FontSystem`. Monospace fallback caching. Tab width support.
**v0.14**: Letter spacing, variable font features, configurable font fallback.
**v0.15**: **ASCII fast-path** skips complex shaping/bidi for ASCII runs — significant perf win for terminals. `rustybuzz` replaced by HarfRust (pure Rust). `set_text`/`set_rich_text` gain alignment param.
**v0.16**: Monospace width rounding fix ensures grid alignment. New `Renderer` trait.
**v0.18**: Ellipsizing support.

## sysinfo 0.33 → 0.38 (Low effort)

**v0.34**: `multithread` feature now opt-in. Dead processes auto-removed on refresh. Windows: dropped `NtQueryInformationProcess` (fixes AV false positives).
**v0.35**: `kill_and_wait()` added. MSRV 1.75.
**v0.37**: MSRV 1.88 (Rust 2024 edition). Improved partial process retrieval perf on Linux.
No process-tree API breakage — straightforward upgrade.

## Suggested order

1. **sysinfo** (quick win, isolated to therminal-terminal)
2. **cosmic-text + glyphon** together (coupled, best ROI from ASCII fast-path)
3. **wgpu + glyphon** together (biggest lift, touches render pipeline)
4. **Remaining 4** (notify, dirs, portable-pty, toml, pollster — all low effort)","design":"Upgrade order matters because wgpu, cosmic-text, and glyphon are tightly coupled through the text rendering pipeline. cosmic-text feeds shaped glyphs to glyphon which renders via wgpu. Upgrading cosmic-text alone may work if glyphon 0.7 is compatible, but glyphon 0.10 likely requires wgpu 29. Check glyphon's wgpu version requirements before deciding whether to do cosmic-text alone or bundle all three.

MSRV will need to go to at least 1.88 (sysinfo 0.37 requirement). Verify CI toolchain version.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"marcinek.matthew@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-04-06T05:47:32Z","created_by":"GGPrompts","updated_at":"2026-04-06T05:47:32Z","labels":["dependencies","tech-debt"]}

{"id":"tn-2zo9","title":"Client-side decorations: custom title bar with tabs and settings menu","description":"Replace OS-native window decorations with a custom GPU-rendered title bar, giving therminal a consistent cross-platform look and enabling tabs-in-titlebar UX similar to Windows Terminal.

## What
- Disable OS window decorations (`window.set_decorations(false)`) via winit
- Render a custom title bar with: window drag area, minimize/maximize/close buttons, workspace tabs (move from current tab bar), settings/hamburger menu button
- Implement window drag (on title bar click-drag), double-click to maximize/restore
- Platform-specific: respect macOS traffic light positioning (left side), Windows/Linux close button positioning (right side)
- Settings menu dropdown: font size, theme, config file open, about

## Why
- Consistent cross-platform appearance — same chrome on Windows, Linux, macOS
- Tabs-in-titlebar saves vertical space (merge current tab bar into title bar)
- Settings menu provides discoverability for config options without requiring manual TOML editing
- Professional terminal emulators (Windows Terminal, iTerm2, Warp) all use custom chrome

## Considerations
- Window dragging must feel native (no lag, correct hit-testing)
- macOS has strong conventions around traffic light position and full-screen behavior
- Wayland CSD is the norm; X11 needs careful handling of WM hints
- Config toggle: `general.use_custom_titlebar` (default true) with fallback to OS decorations
- Accessibility: title bar buttons need proper contrast and sizing","notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
Therminal currently uses OS window decorations (default winit). A custom CSD title bar would integrate tabs directly into the title bar (like VS Code / modern terminals), save vertical space, and give full control over the chrome appearance.

## Task
Implement client-side decorations with custom title bar:

1. Set `.with_decorations(false)` on window creation in `window/mod.rs` `resumed()` (~line 1116)
2. Extend the existing tab bar (chrome.rs `draw_tab_bar()` ~line 971) to serve as the title bar:
   - Add window control buttons (minimize, maximize/restore, close) on the right
   - Make the tab bar area draggable for window moving (winit `drag_window()`)
   - Double-click on empty tab bar area = toggle maximize
3. Add hit-testing in `window/mouse.rs` for the new control buttons
4. Handle platform differences: on macOS traffic lights go left, on Windows/Linux controls go right
5. Add `general.use_csd` config option (default: true on Linux, false on macOS) in TherminalConfig
6. When CSD is disabled, fall back to OS decorations (current behavior)
7. Adjust geometry constants: TAB_BAR_HEIGHT may need to increase to accommodate window controls

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/mod.rs` — window creation, `.with_decorations()`

{"id":"tn-9dol","title":"Fix directional pane navigation (spatial) and audit close-double-fire","description":"Ctrl+Shift+Arrow keys don't navigate spatially between panes — they're aliased to index-based cycling (FocusNext/FocusPrev). User also reports two panes closing at once occasionally.

## Bug 1: Directional keys aliased to cycling (CRITICAL)

In `crates/therminal-app/src/window/mod.rs:690-694`, FocusUp/Down/Left/Right are grouped with FocusNext/FocusPrev:

```rust
KeyAction::FocusNext | KeyAction::FocusRight | KeyAction::FocusDown => {
    self.move_focus(FocusDirection::Next);
}
KeyAction::FocusPrev | KeyAction::FocusLeft | KeyAction::FocusUp => {
    self.move_focus(FocusDirection::Prev);
}
```

Pressing Ctrl+Shift+Right should move to the pane *to the right*, not the next pane in tree order.

## Bug 2: Cycling order is tree-order, not spatial

`adjacent_pane()` in `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/layout.rs:276-287` walks pane IDs in tree traversal order. In a 2x2 grid this causes unpredictable focus jumps.

## Bug 3 (investigate): Two panes closing at once

User reports closing one pane sometimes closes two. Close logic in `pane_ops.rs` looks safe (remove_pane returns early if pane missing, take() guards on_exit callback). May be a visual artifact of focus shifting unexpectedly due to Bug 1/2, or a separate race. Needs reproduction.

## Files involved

- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/mod.rs` — dispatch (lines 690-694)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/layout.rs` — adjacent_pane() (lines 271-288)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs` — close logic (lines 116-243)
- `crates/therminal-core/src/config/keybindings.rs` — keybinding definitions (correct, not the issue)","design":"1. Add spatial variants to FocusDirection (Up/Down/Left/Right) or a separate SpatialDirection enum.
2. Implement `spatial_adjacent_pane()` on the layout that computes pane viewport rects and finds the nearest neighbor in the requested direction.
3. Separate dispatch: FocusNext/FocusPrev → index cycling, FocusUp/Down/Left/Right → spatial lookup.
4. For the double-close: add tracing/logging around close_pane_by_id to capture if it fires twice, then reproduce and fix if confirmed.","acceptance_criteria":"- Ctrl+Shift+Right moves focus to the pane visually to the right of the current pane
- Ctrl+Shift+Left/Up/Down behave spatially as expected
- FocusNext/FocusPrev (Ctrl+Shift+N/P) still cycle in order
- Closing a single pane never closes a second pane
- Works correctly with 2, 3, and 4+ pane layouts
- Existing pane tests pass, new tests cover spatial navigation","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"marcinek.matthew@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-04-06T06:04:37Z","created_by":"GGPrompts","updated_at":"2026-04-06T06:04:37Z","labels":["keybindings","pane-management"]}

new_value: Fixed spatial pane navigation: added SpatialDirection enum and spatial_adjacent_pane() using viewport center-point distance with 2x cross-axis penalty. Separated dispatch so FocusUp/Down/Left/Right use spatial lookup while FocusNext/FocusPrev use cycling. Added tracing to close_pane_by_id for double-close investigation. 7 new spatial navigation tests covering 2/3/4/5-pane layouts. Also fixed pre-existing clippy errors in therminal-daemon MCP code.

{"notes":"## Retro
- What worked: Clean separation of SpatialDirection enum from FocusDirection kept the cycling logic untouched. The 2x cross-axis penalty in distance scoring correctly handles L-shaped and asymmetric layouts.
- What was unclear: Bug 3 (double-close) -- no reproduction path found. The on_exit.take() pattern in spawn.rs prevents duplicate PaneExited events per pane. Added detailed tracing (pane_count_before/after, focused pane state) to close_pane_by_id to capture future occurrences. The most likely cause is two panes exiting near-simultaneously (e.g., shell group), which is correct behavior.
## Root cause found for double-close bug

No repeat filtering on key events. At `window/mod.rs:1542-1546`, the match filters `ElementState::Pressed` but does NOT check `key_event.repeat`. Winit fires repeated `Pressed` events when a key is held, so holding Ctrl+Shift+W even briefly fires ClosePane multiple times.

Fix: either filter `!key_event.repeat` globally at line 1545, or selectively for destructive actions (ClosePane, CloseAllPanes, CloseWindow). Global filter is simpler and matches how most apps work — keybinding actions don't auto-repeat."}

{"notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
The app has WorkspaceManager with named workspaces (1-9) and layout trees, but the daemon has no concept of workspaces — it only knows sessions. For MCP tools like list_workspaces to work, the daemon needs workspace awareness.

## Task
Sync workspace/tab state between app and daemon:

1. Add workspace concept to daemon's Session model: `workspace_id`, `workspace_name`, `workspace_order`
2. Add IPC messages for workspace sync: `SetWorkspaceState`, `GetWorkspaces` in `therminal-protocol/src/daemon.rs`
3. App sends workspace changes to daemon: on workspace switch, create, rename, pane move
4. Daemon tracks workspace topology so MCP tools can query it without app being connected
5. Consider: should daemon own workspace state (source of truth) or mirror app state?
   - Recommendation: daemon owns it, app syncs on attach. This way headless/daemon-only sessions have workspaces too.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — add Workspace struct to Session model
- `crates/therminal-protocol/src/daemon.rs` — new IPC request/response variants
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/workspace.rs` — sync workspace changes to daemon
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/server.rs` — handle new IPC messages

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-9ub --reason "Workspace state synced with daemon"`
## Retro
- What worked: Clean separation of concerns -- wire types in protocol crate, storage in daemon session manager, export method in app workspace manager. The serde(default) on new PersistedSession fields ensures backward compatibility with existing sessions.json files.
- What was unclear: The app currently has no daemon IPC client connection during normal operation (only MCP stdio bridge exists). The actual sync calls from app event loop to daemon will need a future issue to wire up a persistent DaemonClient in the App struct. The protocol and daemon-side handling are complete and ready."}

{"id":"tn-k6y","title":"MCP Resources: expose pane content as subscription-based resources","description":"Implement MCP Resources (not just tools) so clients can subscribe to pane content updates rather than polling. rmcp supports the Resources protocol.\n\nResources to expose:\n- pane://{pane_id}/content — live pane content\n- pane://{pane_id}/regions — semantic region updates\n- session://{session_id}/events — session event stream\n\nThis is the subscription-based complement to the request-response tools.","notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
MCP Resources allow subscription-based access to pane content — agents can subscribe to a pane's output stream rather than polling read_pane_content. This is the reactive counterpart to the request/response tools.

## Task
Implement MCP Resources for pane content:

1. Define resource URIs: `terminal://pane/{pane_id}/content` for current grid, `terminal://pane/{pane_id}/output` for streaming output
2. Implement resource listing: return available pane resources from SessionManager
3. Implement resource reading: snapshot current grid content (like read_pane_content but as a resource)
4. Implement resource subscriptions: use DaemonEvent::PaneOutput broadcast channel to push updates to subscribed clients
5. Check rmcp crate's resource/subscription API — the existing MCP server in mcp.rs may need additional trait impls
6. Trust tier: Observer for reading, apply same enforcement as tools

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs` — add resource handlers alongside tool handlers
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — event_tx for output streaming
- rmcp crate docs for Resource trait implementation

## Context
The MCP stdio bridge already handles Windows named pipes on the client side (mcp_stdio.rs:70-100). The socket path resolves to `\\.\pipe\therminal-mcp` on Windows. But the daemon's MCP server only listens on Unix sockets — so MCP doesn't work on Windows native builds.

## Task
Add a `#[cfg(windows)]` branch in `start_mcp_server()` that:
1. Uses `tokio::net::windows::named_pipe::ServerOptions` to create a named pipe
2. Accepts connections in a loop, spawning a new pipe instance after each accept (standard Windows named pipe server pattern)
3. Hands each connection to the same `TherminalMcpServer` handler via rmcp
4. Mirror the Unix path's permission setting, cleanup-on-shutdown, and stale-socket removal logic

Reference the existing Windows client code in mcp_stdio.rs:70-100 for the tokio named pipe API.

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs (~line 1560, start_mcp_server)
- crates/therminal-app/src/mcp_stdio.rs (lines 70-100, client-side reference)
- crates/therminal-runtime/src/paths.rs (socket_path, line 97)

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-68ma --reason "Daemon MCP server listens on Windows named pipes"`","priority":2}

## Context
Therminal already supports manual split panes via keybindings. When AI agents spawn subprocesses (e.g., Claude Code launching parallel workers), users must manually create panes. This should be automatic — the agent registry (tn-5oh) provides the "who spawned" events, this issue provides the "auto-arrange" response.

## Task

2. On agent spawn: auto-split the parent pane to create a new pane for the child agent
3. On agent exit: reclaim the pane space (merge back into parent or redistribute)
4. Use the existing LayoutNode binary tree and split_pane/close_pane logic
5. Add a config toggle `general.auto_tile = true` (default on)
6. Debounce rapid spawn/exit cycles (e.g., 200ms) to avoid layout thrashing

- crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs (close/split operations)

{"id":"tn-vuo","title":"Auto-tiling: dynamic pane arrangement when subagents spawn/finish","description":"When the agent detector notices new subagent processes spawning (e.g., Claude Code spawning parallel workers), automatically create and arrange panes. When agents finish, reclaim space.\n\nBuild on the existing LayoutNode binary tree and auto-tile split logic. The key new behavior is event-driven layout changes triggered by process tree changes rather than manual keybindings.","notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
Therminal already supports manual split panes via keybindings. When AI agents spawn subprocesses (e.g., Claude Code launching parallel workers), users must manually create panes. This should be automatic — the agent registry (tn-5oh) provides the "who spawned" events, this issue provides the "auto-arrange" response.

## Task
1. Subscribe to AgentRegistry events (from tn-5oh) in the app's event loop
2. On agent spawn: auto-split the parent pane to create a new pane for the child agent
3. On agent exit: reclaim the pane space (merge back into parent or redistribute)
4. Use the existing LayoutNode binary tree and split_pane/close_pane logic
5. Add a config toggle `general.auto_tile = true` (default on)
6. Debounce rapid spawn/exit cycles (e.g., 200ms) to avoid layout thrashing

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/layout.rs (LayoutNode, split logic)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/spawn.rs (pane creation)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs (close/split operations)

{"description":"When auto-tiling spawns a new agent pane, it currently bisects the parent pane — which quickly produces tiny unusable panes with multiple agents. Instead, replicate WM-style (Hyprland) behavior:

1. Find the largest pane by area and split that one, not the parent
2. Enforce minimum viable pane size (e.g., 40 cols x 10 rows) — refuse to split below this
3. On agent exit, compact the layout: remove the pane and rebalance the tree to fill gaps

This is the equivalent of what thermal-desktop gets for free from Hyprland's tiling WM.","notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
Auto-tiling (tn-vuo) splits the parent pane when an agent spawns. This makes nested splits tiny fast. Hyprland-style WMs solve this by always splitting the largest available space. therminal needs the same behavior.

## Task
1. Add a `find_largest_pane()` method to `LayoutNode` that returns the pane ID with the largest viewport area
2. Modify `AutoTileDebouncer` / `poll_auto_tile()` in pane_ops.rs to split the largest pane instead of the parent
3. Add minimum pane size enforcement: `MIN_SPLIT_COLS = 40`, `MIN_SPLIT_ROWS = 10` — if splitting the largest pane would produce panes below this, refuse to split
4. Add `compact_layout()` to LayoutNode that removes Empty leaves and rebalances split ratios
5. Wire compact_layout into the Reclaim action path (when auto-tiled panes are closed)

{"notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
Right-click context menu always shows on pane content, conflicting with TUI apps that have their own menus (btop, lazygit, etc.). tmux handles this by checking if the app has mouse reporting enabled — if so, pass through; if not, show tmux's menu. Also, tab-specific actions (Close Tab, Rename Tab) have no UI — a tab bar right-click menu would fix that.

## Task
### Part 1: Smart mouse pass-through
1. In `mouse.rs`, the `pane_term_mode()` function (~line 515) already checks TermMode. In the right-click handler in `mod.rs` (~line 1418), check if the focused pane has mouse reporting enabled (TermMode contains MOUSE_REPORT_CLICK or SGR_MOUSE)
2. If mouse reporting is ON: encode the right-click as a mouse event and send to the PTY (pass-through to TUI)
3. If mouse reporting is OFF: show therminal's context menu (current behavior)

### Part 2: Tab bar right-click menu
4. In `chrome.rs` `tab_bar_hit_test()` (~line 1228), detect which tab was right-clicked
5. Add a new `MenuContext::Tab { workspace_id }` variant to menu.rs
6. Add `build_tab_menu()` in menu.rs with items:
   - Close Tab (KeyAction::CloseAllPanes)
   - Rename Tab (new KeyAction::RenameWorkspace — add to keybindings.rs)
   - New Tab (new KeyAction::NewWorkspace)
   - separator
   - Split Horizontal / Split Vertical (operate on focused pane in that tab)
7. Wire tab right-click in the mouse handler to open this menu

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/mouse.rs (lines 282-285 mouse_mode check, 515-537 pane_term_mode)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/mod.rs (lines 903-908 open_context_menu, 1418-1427 right-click handler)
- crates/therminal-app/src/menu.rs (MenuContext enum, build_pane_menu, add build_tab_menu)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/chrome.rs (line 1228 tab_bar_hit_test)
- crates/therminal-core/src/config/keybindings.rs (KeyAction enum — add RenameWorkspace, NewWorkspace)

{"notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
Users want to resize individual panes without changing font size. Mouse drag on separators already adjusts split_ratio, but there's no keyboard equivalent. Ctrl+Shift+=/-/0 would nudge the focused pane's parent split ratio, matching the Ctrl+=/-/0 pattern for font size.

## Task
1. Add three new `KeyAction` variants in `keybindings.rs`: `ResizePaneGrow`, `ResizePaneShrink`, `ResizePaneReset`
2. Add default keybindings: `ctrl+shift+=` → ResizePaneGrow, `ctrl+shift+-` → ResizePaneShrink, `ctrl+shift+0` → ResizePaneReset
3. Add `find_parent_split(&self, pane_id: PaneId) -> Option<&mut Split>` or equivalent to LayoutNode — find the Split node whose direct child contains the given pane
4. Implement `resize_pane_ratio(pane_id, delta: f32)` in pane_ops.rs:
   - Walk layout tree to find the Split node containing the focused pane as a direct child
   - If focused pane is `first`, increase ratio by delta (grow = more space). If `second`, decrease ratio
   - Clamp to 0.1..0.9 (same as mouse drag in mouse.rs ~line 752)
5. Implement `reset_all_ratios()` in LayoutNode — recursively set all split ratios to 0.5
6. Wire the three actions in `handle_keybinding()` in mod.rs (~line 950)
7. Add help overlay entries for the new keybindings

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-core/src/config/keybindings.rs (KeyAction enum, default_keybindings)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/layout.rs (LayoutNode Split variant with ratio, add find_parent_split + reset_all_ratios)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs (add resize_pane_ratio, reset_all_ratios methods on App)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/mod.rs (~line 950, handle_keybinding dispatch)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/mouse.rs (line 752, reference for ratio clamping logic)

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-q3nx --reason "Pane resize keybindings implemented"`"}

{"id":"tn-q3nx","title":"Pane resize keybindings: Ctrl+Shift+=/-/0 to adjust split ratio","description":"Add keybindings to resize the focused pane by adjusting the parent split ratio:

- Ctrl+Shift+= — grow focused pane (increase its share of the split)
- Ctrl+Shift+- — shrink focused pane (decrease its share)
- Ctrl+Shift+0 — reset all split ratios to 0.5 (equal)

This allows resizing individual panes without changing font size. Useful when different TUIs have different minimum column/row requirements (e.g., shrinking btop without making everything else tiny).

The split ratio adjustment already works via mouse drag on separators. This just adds keyboard control with a fixed step (e.g., 0.05 per keypress).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"marcinek.matthew@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-04-06T16:36:57Z","created_by":"GGPrompts","updated_at":"2026-04-06T16:36:57Z","labels":["keybindings","phase-3","ux"]}

{"notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
Clipboard initialization in clipboard.rs:67-71 panics when both clipboard init attempts fail. This crashes the terminal in headless environments, CI, or broken desktop sessions. The code has a comment saying "essentially unreachable" but it IS reachable — tests hit it in CI.

## Task
1. In `clipboard.rs` line 67-71, replace the `panic!` with a graceful fallback. The `get_clipboard()` function returns `Option<&Mutex<Clipboard>>` — change the double-failure path to return the OnceLock with a value that always errors on actual clipboard operations, OR change `get_clipboard()` to handle the case where OnceLock holds no valid clipboard
2. Simplest fix: make `get_clipboard()` return `None` when clipboard is unavailable instead of panicking. Change the OnceLock pattern to `OnceLock<Option<Mutex<arboard::Clipboard>>>` or use a separate `OnceLock<bool>` to track initialization failure
3. Ensure `copy_to_clipboard()` and `paste_from_clipboard()` gracefully handle `None` with a `tracing::warn!` instead of crashing
4. Fix the test `parse_osc52_recognised` to not depend on a real clipboard — it should only test OSC 52 parsing, not clipboard write

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/clipboard.rs (lines 20-72, get_clipboard + panic path)

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-kzeh --reason "Clipboard gracefully degrades in headless environments"`"}

{"notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
In client.rs, `send_request()` uses `tokio::time::timeout()` at line ~204. When timeout triggers, the request_id stays in the `pending` HashMap forever. The entry is only removed when a response arrives (line ~299 in the reader task). For hung requests, this leaks memory and causes misleading late-response warnings.

## Task
1. In the reader/writer task (around line 276 where `pending` is defined), add cleanup for timed-out requests
2. The cleanest approach: after `send_request()` timeout fires (line 206), send a cancellation command through the writer channel that removes the request_id from pending. OR use a separate cleanup mechanism
3. Alternative simpler approach: in the WriterCmd::Send handler, store the request_id. After timeout, have `send_request` remove the entry from pending directly (it has access to pending via Arc clone)
4. Handle late responses for cleaned-up requests gracefully — if a response arrives for a removed request_id, just drop it silently (no warn)
5. Add a test that verifies pending map is cleaned up after timeout

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/client.rs (lines 190-208 send_request, 276 pending map, ~299 reader task)

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-hjxm --reason "Pending requests cleaned up on timeout"`"}

Under repeated slow or hung requests, stale entries accumulate and late responses become misleading.

Impact:

- Confusing late-response handling and warning noise.

Likely file:
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/client.rs","acceptance_criteria":"- When a request times out, its pending entry is removed promptly.

{"notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
In persistence.rs:57, `save_sync()` uses plain `std::fs::write()` which is non-atomic — a crash mid-write corrupts sessions.json. The correct temp-file-then-rename pattern already exists in `state_inference/persistence.rs:27-40`.

## Task
1. In persistence.rs `save_sync()` (~line 48), replace `std::fs::write(&path, json)` with the atomic write pattern:
   - Write to `path.with_extension("json.tmp")`
   - `std::fs::rename(tmp_path, path)` (atomic on POSIX, nearly-atomic on Windows)
   - Clean up tmp file on rename failure
2. Reference the existing implementation in `crates/therminal-terminal/src/state_inference/persistence.rs:27-40` for the exact pattern
3. Add a test that verifies the temp file is cleaned up after successful write

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/persistence.rs (line 57, save_sync)
- crates/therminal-terminal/src/state_inference/persistence.rs (lines 27-40, reference pattern)

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-6mp4 --reason "Session persistence uses atomic writes"`"}

{"id":"tn-6mp4","title":"Make daemon session persistence writes atomic","description":"Code review finding: daemon persistence writes `sessions.json` with plain `std::fs::write`, while another part of the codebase already uses a safer temp-file-then-rename pattern. A crash or power loss during write can leave restore state truncated or corrupt.

Impact:
- Sessions may fail to restore after interrupted writes.
- Reliability gap in daemon shutdown/save path.

Likely files:
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/persistence.rs
- crates/therminal-terminal/src/state_inference/persistence.rs","acceptance_criteria":"- `sessions.json` writes use an atomic temp-file-and-rename strategy.
- Interrupted or failed writes do not replace a previously valid persistence file.
- Add a focused test for the persistence write helper if practical.","notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
In persistence.rs:57, `save_sync()` uses plain `std::fs::write()` which is non-atomic — a crash mid-write corrupts sessions.json. The correct temp-file-then-rename pattern already exists in `state_inference/persistence.rs:27-40`.

## Task
1. In persistence.rs `save_sync()` (~line 48), replace `std::fs::write(&path, json)` with the atomic write pattern:
   - Write to `path.with_extension("json.tmp")`
   - `std::fs::rename(tmp_path, path)` (atomic on POSIX, nearly-atomic on Windows)
   - Clean up tmp file on rename failure
2. Reference the existing implementation in `crates/therminal-terminal/src/state_inference/persistence.rs:27-40` for the exact pattern
3. Add a test that verifies the temp file is cleaned up after successful write

- crates/therminal-terminal/src/state_inference/persistence.rs (lines 27-40, reference pattern)

{"id":"tn-q3nx","title":"Pane resize keybindings: Ctrl+Shift+=/-/0 to adjust split ratio","description":"Add keybindings to resize the focused pane by adjusting the parent split ratio:

- Ctrl+Shift+= — grow focused pane (increase its share of the split)
- Ctrl+Shift+- — shrink focused pane (decrease its share)
- Ctrl+Shift+0 — reset all split ratios to 0.5 (equal)

This allows resizing individual panes without changing font size. Useful when different TUIs have different minimum column/row requirements (e.g., shrinking btop without making everything else tiny).

The split ratio adjustment already works via mouse drag on separators. This just adds keyboard control with a fixed step (e.g., 0.05 per keypress).","notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
Users want to resize individual panes without changing font size. Mouse drag on separators already adjusts split_ratio, but there's no keyboard equivalent. Ctrl+Shift+=/-/0 would nudge the focused pane's parent split ratio, matching the Ctrl+=/-/0 pattern for font size.

## Task
1. Add three new `KeyAction` variants in `keybindings.rs`: `ResizePaneGrow`, `ResizePaneShrink`, `ResizePaneReset`
2. Add default keybindings: `ctrl+shift+=` → ResizePaneGrow, `ctrl+shift+-` → ResizePaneShrink, `ctrl+shift+0` → ResizePaneReset
3. Add `find_parent_split(&self, pane_id: PaneId) -> Option<&mut Split>` or equivalent to LayoutNode — find the Split node whose direct child contains the given pane
4. Implement `resize_pane_ratio(pane_id, delta: f32)` in pane_ops.rs:
   - Walk layout tree to find the Split node containing the focused pane as a direct child
   - If focused pane is `first`, increase ratio by delta (grow = more space). If `second`, decrease ratio
   - Clamp to 0.1..0.9 (same as mouse drag in mouse.rs ~line 752)
5. Implement `reset_all_ratios()` in LayoutNode — recursively set all split ratios to 0.5
6. Wire the three actions in `handle_keybinding()` in mod.rs (~line 950)

Terminal emulators like Windows Terminal, iTerm2, and Kitty provide visual/audible/taskbar notifications on BEL and support richer notification protocols.

## What to implement

### Phase 1 — BEL handling (low-hanging fruit)

- Add optional visual bell (brief screen flash/invert) as a config option

### Phase 2 — OSC 9 desktop notifications
- Parse `\e]9;text\a` in `SequenceInterceptor` 
- Route to OS notification system (`notify-rust` on Linux, Windows toast API on Windows, macOS Notification Center)

### Phase 3 — Agent-aware notifications (unique to Therminal)
- When `AgentStatus` transitions to `AwaitingInput`, auto-trigger a notification even if no BEL was emitted
- Configurable: `[notifications] agent_waiting = true`
- Include agent name and pane context in the notification
- This is a differentiating feature — no other terminal can do this since they don't track agent state

{"notes":"## prepared.prompt

## Context
The render loop has quadratic hotspot/URL application work that causes frame-time jitter on dense panes. Three specific O(n*m) patterns identified in code review.

## Task
Fix three quadratic patterns in the render path:

### 1. URL copy-back: O(N*M) linear search (render.rs:298-307)
The loop finds matching cells via `cells.iter_mut().find()` for each damaged cell with a hyperlink. Replace with a HashMap keyed by `(row, col)` for O(1) lookup.

### 2. Hotspot application: O(H*C) nested loop (render.rs:310-319, 325-334)
For each hotspot, rescans entire cell list to find cells in range. Instead, build a row-indexed structure: `HashMap<usize, Vec<&Hotspot>>` keyed by row, then single-pass over cells checking only hotspots on the same row.

### 3. Hotspot map rebuild: O(C) every frame (grid_renderer.rs:1042-1071)
Rebuilds `hotspot_map` by scanning ALL visible cells even when only a few rows are damaged. Only update entries for damaged rows, remove stale entries for those rows first.

### Bonus
4. Consider `Cow<>` or in-place mutation to avoid the cell clone at render.rs:287-291.

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/render.rs:287-334 (cell clone, URL copy, hotspot apply)
- crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs:801-816 (row cache update), 1042-1071 (hotspot map rebuild)

## Context
Therminal needs a widget plugin architecture to keep harness-specific integrations (Claude context gauge, Codex status, etc.) out of core. The detection layer (agent registry, state inference, cadence analysis) is agent-agnostic; only the rendering of that data needs to be extensible.

## Task
1. Define a `Widget` trait in a new module `crates/therminal-app/src/widgets/mod.rs`:
   ```rust
   pub trait Widget: Send {
       fn id(&self) -> &str;
       fn on_agent_event(&mut self, event: &AgentEvent);
       fn on_state_change(&mut self, notification: &StateChangeNotification);
       fn needs_redraw(&self) -> bool;
       fn layout(&self, pane_viewport: Rect, cell_metrics: CellMetrics) -> WidgetRect;
       fn render(&mut self, ctx: &mut WidgetRenderContext) -> Vec<ColorVertex>;
   }
   ```
2. Create `WidgetRenderContext` that wraps access to `GridRenderer`'s overlay atlas, rect pipeline, and `pixel_rect_to_ndc()` helper
3. Create `WidgetManager` that:
   - Holds `Vec<Box<dyn Widget>>`
   - Subscribes to `AgentEvent` channel (via `AgentRegistry::take_event_rx()`)
   - Dispatches events to all registered widgets
   - Calls `render()` only on widgets where `needs_redraw()` returns true
4. Add `[widgets]` config section to `TherminalConfig` for enabling/disabling widgets by id
5. Wire `WidgetManager` into the render loop after the grid pass (in the overlay pass from tn-9k2)
6. Implement one example built-in widget (agent status badge) as proof of concept

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-terminal/src/agent_registry.rs:81-96 (AgentEvent), 115 (take_event_rx)

{"id":"tn-8hdt","title":"Widget plugin/extension architecture","description":"Design and implement a plugin system for overlay widgets, keeping harness-specific integrations out of core therminal.

## Motivation
The codebase is already large. Adding harness-specific widgets (Claude context gauge, Codex status, etc.) to core would be unmaintainable as the agent ecosystem grows. Instead, expose a clean internal API that plugins consume.

## Design
1. **Widget trait** — plugins implement update(AgentState) + render() -> OverlayQuads
2. **Event subscription** — widgets subscribe to AgentEvent from the agent registry (already has mpsc channel)
3. **Layout contract** — widget declares preferred position/size, overlay system places it respecting pane geometry
4. **Loading** — config-driven plugin dir or built-in registry, feature-flag gated
5. **Responsive** — widgets adapt to pane size via the layout contract, not custom logic

## Scope boundary
- Core therminal: detection layer (agent registry, state inference, cadence) + overlay rendering infrastructure (two-pass GPU, layout)
- Plugins: specific widgets (context gauge, tool cards, thinking indicator, result cards)
- Plugins can also be exposed as AI skills via MCP

## Dependencies
Depends on tn-9k2 (two-pass GPU rendering) for the overlay surface.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"marcinek.matthew@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-04-06T19:34:21Z","created_by":"GGPrompts","updated_at":"2026-04-06T19:34:21Z","labels":["architecture","phase-6"]}

## Context
The OSC 7777 cooperative agent self-reporting protocol is fully implemented in therminal (interceptor.rs). It needs to be published as an open specification so other terminals and AI agents can adopt it.

## Task
1. Create `docs/osc-7777-spec.md` documenting the protocol:
   - Sequence format: `ESC ] 7777 ; {json} ST`
   - JSON schema with required and optional fields:
     - `agent` (string, required): agent identifier
     - `state` (string, required): one of idle, processing, streaming, tool_use, awaiting_input, thinking
     - `tool` (string, optional): current tool name when state=tool_use
     - `tokens` (number, optional): context usage percentage (0-100)
     - `model` (string, optional): model identifier
   - Intended semantics for each state
   - Examples of valid sequences
   - Rationale for choosing 7777 (no known conflicts with existing terminals)
2. Reference the implementation in `crates/therminal-terminal/src/interceptor.rs` as the reference parser
3. Include a "For Terminal Implementors" section explaining how to consume the protocol
4. Include a "For Agent Implementors" section explaining how to emit the protocol

{"id":"tn-xvwv","title":"Add subagent JSONL discovery + per-subagent tailers","description":"Port the subagent discovery slice of swarm_watcher.rs. For each active parent session, poll ~/.claude/projects/{hash}/{parent-sid}/subagents/ for agent-*.jsonl files and spawn a SessionJsonlTailer for each. Treat subagents as first-class sessions with parent_session_id set.

## Why
Subagents (Task tool invocations) run inside the parent claude process — invisible to the OS process tree. The only way to observe them is via their dedicated JSONL files. This is what makes 'what is the agent actually doing right now' visible.

## Scope
- Poll .../subagents/ dirs on each tick (simple; upgrade to notify later if latency matters)
- Spawn one SessionJsonlTailer per new agent-*.jsonl
- Tag emitted events with parent_session_id and agent_id from the JSONL envelope (isSidechain, parentUuid, agentId fields)
- Clean up tailers when parent session ends

## Acceptance
- Fire a Task tool in a claude pane, see subagent AgentEvents with parent_session_id set in daemon logs
- Multiple concurrent subagents tracked independently
- No leaked tailers after parent session closes

## References
- ~/projects/thermal-desktop/crates/thermal-conductor/src/swarm_watcher.rs (lines 97, 120-124, 316-319)
- Real subagent JSONL example: ~/.claude/projects/-home-marci-projects-ggl/09364758.../subagents/agent-a2834e759e4f02c68.jsonl","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"marcinek.matthew@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-04-06T23:44:00Z","created_by":"GGPrompts","updated_at":"2026-04-06T23:44:00Z"}

{"id":"tn-uzrc","title":"CSD window edge resize handles + cursor affordances","description":"When general.use_csd = true (default on Linux + Windows), therminal creates a borderless winit window via with_decorations(false). This leaves users with no visible or functional way to resize the main window — there are no OS-provided resize grips, and therminal does not render its own edge affordances.

## Repro
1. Run therminal on Linux (or WSL2) with default config
2. Observe: no window borders, no resize grips, window is not resizable by dragging edges

## Why this exists
use_csd = true is intentional (default_use_csd() in crates/therminal-core/src/config/mod.rs:249) because therminal is supposed to render its own titlebar via the tab bar. But the CSD story is incomplete — only the titlebar piece shipped, not the edge-resize piece.

## Fix
Implement edge resize affordances in crates/therminal-app/src/window/mouse.rs. winit already exposes Window::drag_resize_window(ResizeDirection) for exactly this. Needed:

1. Hit-test a few-pixel border region at all four window edges + four corners. Eight ResizeDirection variants: North, South, East, West, NorthEast, NorthWest, SouthEast, SouthWest.
2. On hover over an edge/corner region: set the appropriate CursorIcon (NResize, SResize, EResize, WResize, NeResize, NwResize, SeResize, SwResize). Follow the pattern used by the existing separator hover code (update_separator_hover in mouse.rs).
3. On mouse button-down in an edge/corner region: call window.drag_resize_window(direction) to hand off interactive resize to the compositor.
4. Integrate cleanly with existing separator / tab-bar / context-menu hit-testing — edge resize should only fire when no other hit target claims the event. Order matters.
5. Respect use_csd = false — if native decorations are on, skip all of this (the OS handles it).
6. Hit tolerance should be small enough not to interfere with clicking terminal content (suggest 4-6 px, matching SEPARATOR_HIT_TOLERANCE).

## Acceptance
- With default config on Linux/Windows, user can grab any edge or corner and resize the window
- Cursor changes to the right N/S/E/W/corner icon on hover
- Does not interfere with pane separator drag, tab bar clicks, or context menu
- macOS unchanged (use_csd defaults false there)

## References
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/mod.rs:1596-1606 (with_decorations(false) call site)
- crates/therminal-core/src/config/mod.rs:249 (default_use_csd)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/mouse.rs (existing separator hit-test + cursor update pattern — mirror this)
- winit Window::drag_resize_window + ResizeDirection

## Workaround for now
Set use_csd = false in therminal.toml — OS decorations come back with normal resize edges.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"marcinek.matthew@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-04-07T00:46:26Z","created_by":"GGPrompts","updated_at":"2026-04-07T00:46:26Z"}

{"id":"tn-bozm","title":"CLI event viewer for therminal://claude/events (dev tool + user-facing)","description":"Build a small CLI binary that subscribes to the therminal://claude/events MCP resource and prints live TaggedAgentEvents as styled terminal lines. Runs in any pane as a lightweight observability surface while waiting for Phase 6 overlay infrastructure.

## Why
The JSONL pipeline (tn-ygcr, shipped 2026-04-06) streams parent + subagent events over MCP but there is no way to see them today. The full GPU timeline widget (tn-x85k) is blocked on Phase 6 overlay primitives (tn-9k2, tn-ojgi) — but the user-facing pain is real NOW: running Claude Code, the user sits and waits while subagents work in silence with zero indication of what they are doing.

A CLI subscriber solves this cheaply. Run it in a second pane, get a live feed. Same tool doubles as:
- Integration test harness for the pipeline
- Reference implementation for 'how do you consume therminal://claude/events'
- Debugging tool that survives any future widget rewrites

## Scope
New binary, probably under crates/therminal-daemon/src/bin/claude-events.rs or a new tiny crate crates/therminal-events-viewer/ — pick whichever fits better given how the daemon IPC client is currently exposed. Read crates/therminal-daemon/src/client.rs first to see if there is already a reusable client API.

Behavior:
1. Connect to the running daemon via the existing IPC socket/named pipe
2. Subscribe to therminal://claude/events via the MCP subscription path
3. For each TaggedAgentEvent received, print one styled line to stdout

Line format (rough):
  HH:MM:SS [top  <sid8>  ]  Bash       ls -la
  HH:MM:SS [top  <sid8>  ]  ✓          (result, dim green)
  HH:MM:SS [sub  <agent8>]  Grep       jsonl_tailer   (indented, different color)
  HH:MM:SS [sub  <agent8>]  ✗          (result with is_error, red)

Color coding (ANSI, no external TUI crate needed):
- Top-level events: default color
- Subagent events: dim/indented
- ToolUse: cyan tool name
- ToolResult success: green
- ToolResult error: red
- Thinking: magenta, truncated
- UserMessage/AssistantMessage: optional, suppressed by default (too noisy), enable with --verbose

Flags:
  --filter top|sub|all    default: all
  --session <sid>         filter to one top-level session and its subagents
  --verbose               include user/assistant messages
  --no-color              disable ANSI
  --json                  raw TaggedAgentEvent JSON per line (for piping to jq)

## Out of scope
- Ratatui or any interactive UI — just streaming println!. Keeping it dead simple.
- Replacing the future timeline widget (tn-x85k).
- Per-session URI filtering on the server side — filter client-side on EventSource.

## Acceptance
- cargo run -p <name> --bin claude-events connects to a running daemon and streams events
- Running claude in a therminal pane produces visible lines in the viewer pane within ~150ms of each event
- Firing a Task tool produces indented subagent lines with different coloring
- --json mode outputs valid JSON lines suitable for jq
- Added to README under a 'Dev tools' or 'Observability' section

## References
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/claude_pipeline.rs (broadcast source)
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/claude_jsonl_tailer.rs (TaggedAgentEvent, EventSource)
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs (subscription protocol, therminal://claude/events URI)
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/client.rs (may already have reusable IPC client code)

## Related
- tn-ygcr: pipeline that produces the events
- tn-x85k: full GPU timeline widget (blocked on Phase 6, this is the stopgap)
- tn-t2sb: settings page (unrelated path, but also surfaces the observability feature)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"marcinek.matthew@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-04-07T00:32:03Z","created_by":"GGPrompts","updated_at":"2026-04-07T00:32:03Z"}

{"id":"tn-bozm","title":"CLI event viewer for therminal://claude/events (dev tool + user-facing)","description":"Build a small CLI binary that subscribes to the therminal://claude/events MCP resource and prints live TaggedAgentEvents as styled terminal lines. Runs in any pane as a lightweight observability surface while waiting for Phase 6 overlay infrastructure.

## Why
The JSONL pipeline (tn-ygcr, shipped 2026-04-06) streams parent + subagent events over MCP but there is no way to see them today. The full GPU timeline widget (tn-x85k) is blocked on Phase 6 overlay primitives (tn-9k2, tn-ojgi) — but the user-facing pain is real NOW: running Claude Code, the user sits and waits while subagents work in silence with zero indication of what they are doing.

A CLI subscriber solves this cheaply. Run it in a second pane, get a live feed. Same tool doubles as:
- Integration test harness for the pipeline
- Reference implementation for 'how do you consume therminal://claude/events'
- Debugging tool that survives any future widget rewrites

## Scope
New binary, probably under crates/therminal-daemon/src/bin/claude-events.rs or a new tiny crate crates/therminal-events-viewer/ — pick whichever fits better given how the daemon IPC client is currently exposed. Read crates/therminal-daemon/src/client.rs first to see if there is already a reusable client API.

Behavior:
1. Connect to the running daemon via the existing IPC socket/named pipe
2. Subscribe to therminal://claude/events via the MCP subscription path
3. For each TaggedAgentEvent received, print one styled line to stdout

Line format (rough):
  HH:MM:SS [top  <sid8>  ]  Bash       ls -la
  HH:MM:SS [top  <sid8>  ]  ✓          (result, dim green)
  HH:MM:SS [sub  <agent8>]  Grep       jsonl_tailer   (indented, different color)
  HH:MM:SS [sub  <agent8>]  ✗          (result with is_error, red)

Color coding (ANSI, no external TUI crate needed):
- Top-level events: default color
- Subagent events: dim/indented
- ToolUse: cyan tool name
- ToolResult success: green
- ToolResult error: red
- Thinking: magenta, truncated
- UserMessage/AssistantMessage: optional, suppressed by default (too noisy), enable with --verbose

Flags:
  --filter top|sub|all    default: all
  --session <sid>         filter to one top-level session and its subagents
  --verbose               include user/assistant messages
  --no-color              disable ANSI
  --json                  raw TaggedAgentEvent JSON per line (for piping to jq)

## Out of scope
- Ratatui or any interactive UI — just streaming println!. Keeping it dead simple.
- Replacing the future timeline widget (tn-x85k).
- Per-session URI filtering on the server side — filter client-side on EventSource.

## Acceptance
- cargo run -p <name> --bin claude-events connects to a running daemon and streams events
- Running claude in a therminal pane produces visible lines in the viewer pane within ~150ms of each event
- Firing a Task tool produces indented subagent lines with different coloring
- --json mode outputs valid JSON lines suitable for jq
- Added to README under a 'Dev tools' or 'Observability' section

## References
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/claude_pipeline.rs (broadcast source)
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/claude_jsonl_tailer.rs (TaggedAgentEvent, EventSource)
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs (subscription protocol, therminal://claude/events URI)
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/client.rs (may already have reusable IPC client code)

## Related
- tn-ygcr: pipeline that produces the events
- tn-x85k: full GPU timeline widget (blocked on Phase 6, this is the stopgap)
- tn-t2sb: settings page (unrelated path, but also surfaces the observability feature)","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"GGPrompts","owner":"marcinek.matthew@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-04-07T00:32:03Z","created_by":"GGPrompts","updated_at":"2026-04-07T01:10:48Z"}

{"id":"tn-0kbo","title":"claude_jsonl_tailer: byte-offset miscount on CRLF files","description":"crates/therminal-daemon/src/claude_jsonl_tailer.rs:194 reconstructs `bytes_consumed` as `line.len() as u64 + 1` (the +1 is for the trailing newline). On Windows with CRLF line endings, BufReader::read_line strips `\r`, so the stored offset is too low by 1 per line. The tailer then re-reads and re-emits the last partial line on every poll cycle on CRLF files.

Fix options:
- After exhausting the BufReader, call `file.stream_position()` to get the true byte offset
- Or use `seek(SeekFrom::Current(0))` after reading
- Or detect and account for the stripped \r explicitly

{"id":"tn-wgs1","title":"claude_pipeline: add shutdown cancellation handle","description":"crates/therminal-daemon/src/claude_pipeline.rs:69 — the pipeline task spawned by spawn_with() runs forever with no cancellation mechanism. There is no select on a shutdown signal.

When the daemon shuts down, the task keeps running until process exit, holding a broadcast::Sender that keeps the channel open. This blocks final cleanup of any subscriber waiting on Closed.

Fix: accept a `shutdown: Arc<Notify>` parameter (same pattern as IpcServer and start_mcp_server_unix) and add `_ = shutdown.notified() => break` to the tick loop. Wire it up in ensure.rs where the pipeline is spawned.

Split crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs (1962 lines) into an mcp/ submodule directory per the CLAUDE.md ~500-line guideline. Pure refactor — no behavioral changes.

This file is the most-edited surface as new MCP tools land, and a recent trust-bypass bug (now fixed) was buried in here precisely because the file was too large to reason about.

**File survey (verify by reading the whole file first):**
- TherminalMcpServer struct + protocol dispatch
- 15 tool handler implementations (handle_*)
- tool_definitions() + call_tool() match dispatch
- Resource listing (list_resources, list_resource_templates)
- Resource reading (read_resource for terminal://pane/{id}/content, terminal://pane/{id}/output, therminal://claude/events)
- Resource subscription management (subscribe/unsubscribe + per-connection ring buffer for claude events)
- start_mcp_server_unix / Windows named pipe transport setup
- spawn_mcp_connection
- cleanup_socket + set_permissions

**Refactor plan:**

1. **READ THE FULL FILE FIRST** — all 1962 lines. The file is dense and the seams aren't obvious from grep alone.
2. Create directory `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/` with:
   - `tools.rs` — all 15 `handle_*` tool methods + `tool_definitions()` + `call_tool()` dispatch
   - `resources.rs` — `list_resources`, `list_resource_templates`, `read_resource`, `subscribe`, `unsubscribe`, plus the Claude events ring-buffer subscription forwarder
   - `transport.rs` — `start_mcp_server_unix`, Windows named pipe variant if present, `spawn_mcp_connection`, `cleanup_socket`, the `set_permissions` call site
   - `mod.rs` — `TherminalMcpServer` struct definition, the MCP protocol dispatch entry points (`initialize`, top-level `call_tool`/`read_resource` glue), shared types/constants
3. **Use `impl TherminalMcpServer` blocks across files** — Rust allows multiple impl blocks for the same struct in the same crate. Each new file declares `use super::TherminalMcpServer;` and `impl TherminalMcpServer { ... }`. No new types or traits.
4. The parent `crates/therminal-daemon/src/lib.rs` (or main.rs) currently has `mod mcp;` or `pub mod mcp;`. Keep that line unchanged.
5. **Delete the old `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs`** after creating the new directory.
6. Tests: if there's a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` in the file, leave it in `mcp/mod.rs`. Don't split tests.

**Critical constraints:**

Working dir: /home/marci/projects/therminal

Split crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs (1962 lines) into an mcp/ submodule directory per the CLAUDE.md ~500-line guideline. Pure refactor — no behavioral changes.

This file is the most-edited surface as new MCP tools land, and a recent trust-bypass bug (now fixed) was buried in here precisely because the file was too large to reason about.

**File survey (verify by reading the whole file first):**
- TherminalMcpServer struct + protocol dispatch
- 15 tool handler implementations (handle_*)
- tool_definitions() + call_tool() match dispatch
- Resource listing (list_resources, list_resource_templates)
- Resource reading (read_resource for terminal://pane/{id}/content, terminal://pane/{id}/output, therminal://claude/events)
- Resource subscription management (subscribe/unsubscribe + per-connection ring buffer for claude events)
- start_mcp_server_unix / Windows named pipe transport setup
- spawn_mcp_connection
- cleanup_socket + set_permissions

**Refactor plan:**

1. **READ THE FULL FILE FIRST** — all 1962 lines. The file is dense and the seams aren't obvious from grep alone.
2. Create directory `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/` with:
   - `tools.rs` — all 15 `handle_*` tool methods + `tool_definitions()` + `call_tool()` dispatch
   - `resources.rs` — `list_resources`, `list_resource_templates`, `read_resource`, `subscribe`, `unsubscribe`, plus the Claude events ring-buffer subscription forwarder
   - `transport.rs` — `start_mcp_server_unix`, Windows named pipe variant if present, `spawn_mcp_connection`, `cleanup_socket`, the `set_permissions` call site
   - `mod.rs` — `TherminalMcpServer` struct definition, the MCP protocol dispatch entry points (`initialize`, top-level `call_tool`/`read_resource` glue), shared types/constants
3. **Use `impl TherminalMcpServer` blocks across files** — Rust allows multiple impl blocks for the same struct in the same crate. Each new file declares `use super::TherminalMcpServer;` and `impl TherminalMcpServer { ... }`. No new types or traits.
4. The parent `crates/therminal-daemon/src/lib.rs` (or main.rs) currently has `mod mcp;` or `pub mod mcp;`. Keep that line unchanged.
5. **Delete the old `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs`** after creating the new directory.
6. Tests: if there's a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` in the file, leave it in `mcp/mod.rs`. Don't split tests.

**Critical constraints:**

- **No behavioral changes.** Trust enforcement, audit logging, ring-buffer semantics — all must be byte-identical. The refactor must not regress the recently-fixed trust-bypass on therminal://claude/events.
- **No new public API.** Items previously `pub` stay `pub`; `pub(crate)` stays `pub(crate)`. Use `pub(super)` for cross-file-but-still-private items.
- **No premature abstraction.** Don't introduce traits like `ToolHandler` or `ResourceHandler` to make the split feel cleaner. Plain methods on the struct.
- Target ~400-700 lines per file.
- Preserve all `#[tracing::instrument]`, audit log calls, and trust check call sites EXACTLY.

**Verify:**
```
cargo fmt --all
cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings 2>&1 | tail -30
cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -E '^test result:' | awk '{ pass+=\$4; fail+=\$6 } END { print \"passed:\",pass,\"failed:\",fail }'
```

All clean. Workspace test count must match the pre-refactor baseline (currently 777). **Pay special attention to the trust.rs tests — they must still pass.**

**When complete:** \`bd close tn-e31n --reason \"mcp.rs split into tools.rs + resources.rs + transport.rs + mod.rs\"\`. - cleanup_socket + set_permissions

   - `mod.rs` — `TherminalMcpServer` struct definition, the MCP protocol dispatch entry points (`initialize`, top-level `call_tool`/`read_resource` glue), shared types/constants
3. **Use `impl TherminalMcpServer` blocks across files** — Rust allows multiple impl blocks for the same struct in the same crate. Each new file declares `use super::TherminalMcpServer;` and `impl TherminalMcpServer { ... }`. No new types or traits.
4. The parent `crates/therminal-daemon/src/lib.rs` (or main.rs) currently has `mod mcp;` or `pub mod mcp;`. Keep that line unchanged.

- **No premature abstraction.** Don't introduce traits like `ToolHandler` or `ResourceHandler` to make the split feel cleaner. Plain methods on the struct.
- Target ~400-700 lines per file.
- Preserve all `#[tracing::instrument]`, audit log calls, and trust check call sites EXACTLY.

**Verify:**
```

Using additional inherent impl blocks across files + pub(super) kept everything private to the module. ServerHandler trait impl stays contiguous in mod.rs and delegates to *_impl inherent methods in resources.rs.
- Line counts: mod 702, tools 791, resources 343, transport 262 (original 1962).
- Verification: cargo fmt, clippy -D warnings clean, 777 tests pass (matches target)."}

- apply_config() (~125L)
- bell handling, keybinding dispatch, NotificationSource handling, active_menu handling
- ApplicationHandler impl, run() entry point

2. Create files under `crates/therminal-app/src/window/`:
   - `init.rs` — `new()` + `init_gpu()` + any startup helpers
   - `event_handler.rs` — the giant `window_event` match arm, broken into per-variant `handle_resize()`, `handle_cursor_moved()`, `handle_mouse_input()`, `handle_keyboard_input()`, etc. Use `pub(super) fn handle_*` methods on `App` (multiple `impl App` blocks across files).
   - `render_driver.rs` — `render()` + `jump_to_region()` + render-related helpers
   - Keep `mod.rs` as the coordinator: App struct definition, ApplicationHandler impl, `run()`, `apply_config()`, NotificationSource enum, and the public surface. Target <500 lines.

   ```rust
   fn window_event(&mut self, event_loop: &ActiveEventLoop, window_id: WindowId, event: WindowEvent) {
       match event {
           WindowEvent::Resized(size) => self.handle_resize(size, event_loop),
           WindowEvent::CursorMoved { .. } => self.handle_cursor_moved(...),
           // ...
       }
   }
   ```
4. Add `mod init; mod event_handler; mod render_driver;` declarations in `mod.rs`.
5. Tests: leave any `#[cfg(test)]` modules where they currently live; if there's one in mod.rs, keep it there.

**Constraints:**

- **No behavioral changes.** Mechanical split. NotificationSource gating, active_menu handling, keybinding dispatch — all preserved exactly.
- **No new public API.** Use `pub(super)` for the new handle_* methods so they're file-crossing-private.
- **No premature abstraction.** No new traits like `EventHandler`. Plain inherent methods on App.
- Target <500 lines for mod.rs; the new files can be 300-600 lines each.
- The chrome submodule already exists (from tn-jl4y) — don't touch chrome/.

**Verify:**
```
cargo fmt --all

```

All clean. Test count must match the wave-1-post-gate baseline.

## Retro
- What worked: Child modules of window/ have access to parent's private items, so no visibility changes were needed for App fields, GpuState, SeparatorDrag, NotificationSource, UserEvent. Methods just moved into new `impl App` blocks under sibling files.
- What was unclear: mod.rs landed at 622L (target was <500). The bell/notification + apply_config + facade methods + ApplicationHandler dispatch all live there. Could be split further (e.g. config_apply.rs, notifications.rs) in a follow-up if desired. event_handler.rs is 787L because the mouse-input arm is itself ~400 lines and was kept as one sub-handler per the issue spec."}

{"description":"The stdio bridge already exists (`therminal-app/src/mcp_stdio.rs`, invoked via `therminal mcp` subcommand) and works on both Unix sockets and Windows named pipes. The user already builds therminal.exe via `scripts/build-windows.{sh,ps1}` and has it deployed on Windows. What's missing is the WSL2 → Windows usage path: a Linux-side MCP client (Claude Code in WSL) launching `/mnt/c/.../therminal.exe mcp` via binfmt_misc interop to drive the daemon running on Windows.

**Why this works at all:** WSL2 binfmt_misc transparently invokes Windows .exe files. `therminal.exe mcp` connects to `\\.\pipe\therminal-mcp` on the Windows side. MCP wire protocol is identical regardless of which side launched the bridge.

**Scope:**
1. Smoke test the path end-to-end on the user's Windows install (the one already built via build-windows.ps1)
2. Document the Claude Code MCP config snippet in `docs/integrations/wsl2.md` — exact path will depend on where build-windows.ps1 deploys (Desktop + %APPDATA%\therminal per CLAUDE.md)
3. Note any gotchas discovered: daemon auto-start on Windows, named pipe permissions across the WSL/Win boundary, working directory handling for `cwd`-using tools, Windows path translation when therminal returns paths to a Linux MCP client

This is mostly verification + docs, not code. Code-only fixes if smoke testing reveals real bugs.

Out of scope: alternative bridge designs (Unix socket via /mnt/wsl, TCP, ssh forward). Stdio is the right transport."}

{"notes":"## prepared.prompt

Extend `terminal.panes.list` to include cwd, last_exit_code, and agent_name. Status bar already displays these via `PaneStatus` — TUIs need the same data.

**Files:**
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/mod.rs` — `PaneInfo` struct (currently has id, session, cols, rows, title)
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/tools.rs` — `handle_list_panes()` populates PaneInfo
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — `Pane` struct holds `cwd: Arc<Mutex<String>>`; check what's stored for last_exit_code and agent_name
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/state.rs` — `PaneStatus` for reference (the in-app version)
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/CLAUDE.md` (or therminal-daemon/CLAUDE.md) — update the tool table

**Task:**
1. Add three optional fields to `PaneInfo`: `cwd: Option<String>`, `last_exit_code: Option<i32>`, `agent_name: Option<String>`. Use Option so the wire format stays backward-compatible — null when unknown.
2. In `handle_list_panes`, look up these fields from the session manager. cwd is already in `Pane`. For last_exit_code and agent_name, check whether the daemon already mirrors this from `AgentRegistry` / OSC 633 events; if not, plumb a minimal accessor — DO NOT replicate the in-app PaneStatus struct, just expose the bits we need.
3. If a piece of data isn't available daemon-side at all (e.g., agent_name lives only in app-side AgentRegistry), document that with a comment and leave the field as None for now. File a follow-up beads issue for the gap.
4. Add tests in `mcp/mod.rs` test module: assert PaneInfo round-trips with all three fields populated and with all None.
5. Update the tool table in `therminal-daemon/CLAUDE.md` to mention the new PaneInfo fields.

**Constraints:**
- Backward compatible: existing clients that don't know about new fields must still work. Use serde defaults / Option.
- No new tools — extend the existing one.
- Trust tier unchanged (Observer).

**Verify:**
```
cargo fmt --all
cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings 2>&1 | tail -30
cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -E '^test result:'
```

**When done:** `mcp__beads__close(issue_id="tn-mriq")` with a summary of what fields were exposed and any gaps documented as follow-ups.","priority":2}

{"notes":"## prepared.prompt

Working dir: /home/marci/projects/therminal

Add MCP tool `terminal.workspaces.get_layout(workspace_id)` returning the binary layout tree + focused_pane. Lets TUIs render layout diagrams and reconstruct splits on reconnect.

**Files:**
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/layout/tree.rs` — `LayoutNode` enum (the source-of-truth tree shape)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/workspace.rs` — `Workspace` struct, focused_pane
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — `WorkspaceInfo` (what daemon currently stores; may need to plumb the LayoutNode tree across)
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/tools.rs` — add `handle_get_workspace_layout`
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/mod.rs` — define result types: `LayoutNodeJson` enum (tagged: `{type: "split", direction, ratio, left, right} | {type: "leaf", pane_id}`)
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/trust.rs` — register tool in `tool_category()` as Observer
- `therminal-daemon/CLAUDE.md` — update tool table

**Task:**
1. Define a serializable mirror of LayoutNode in mcp/mod.rs:
   ```rust
   #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, JsonSchema)]
   #[serde(tag = "type", rename_all = "lowercase")]
   enum LayoutNodeJson {
       Split { direction: String, ratio: f32, left: Box<LayoutNodeJson>, right: Box<LayoutNodeJson> },
       Leaf { pane_id: u64 },
       Empty,
   }
   ```
2. Add a conversion from `LayoutNode` → `LayoutNodeJson` (probably in tree.rs as `pub fn to_json_shape(&self) -> LayoutNodeJson`).
3. The tricky part: the LayoutNode lives in therminal-app, NOT therminal-daemon. The daemon's `WorkspaceInfo` only stores flat pane_ids. **Decide:** either (a) plumb LayoutNode into the daemon's workspace state on attach, or (b) move the conversion into a shared crate. Option (a) is smaller — the app already sends layout snapshots on attach (see `LayoutSnapshot` in pane/layout/snapshot.rs). **Task:**
1. **Read** mcp_stdio.rs (~50 lines visible per earlier context) to confirm: it loads TherminalConfig, resolves the socket path, connects to the daemon endpoint (Unix socket OR Windows named pipe), and copies stdin↔socket bidirectionally.
2. **Write `docs/integrations/wsl2.md`** with these sections:
   - **What this enables**: agents in WSL2 (Claude Code, Codex CLI on Linux) driving therminal running on Windows
   - **Prerequisites**: therminal.exe built and deployed (link to scripts/build-windows.{sh,ps1}); daemon running on Windows (note: depending on auto-start behavior, may need to launch the GUI once)
   - **Find the bridge path**: `which therminal.exe` from WSL after the deploy, or note the deploy locations from build-windows.ps1
   - **Claude Code MCP config snippet** (the canonical use case):
     ```jsonc
     // ~/.claude/mcp.json or project .mcp.json
     {
       "mcpServers": {
         "therminal": {
           "command": "/mnt/c/Users/<you>/AppData/Local/therminal/therminal.exe",
           "args": ["mcp"]
         }
       }
     }
     ```
   - **How it works**: Linux MCP client launches therminal.exe via binfmt_misc → exe runs as a Windows process → connects to `\\.\pipe\therminal-mcp` → forwards JSON-RPC frames over stdin/stdout. The MCP wire protocol is identical regardless of which side launched it.
   - **Gotchas to watch for**: working directory differences (Linux paths vs Windows paths in tool args/results), daemon not running on Windows (the bridge errors out — document the error message), trust tier defaults (Sandboxed by default — the user may need to allowlist the bridge agent in `[trust]` config)
   - **Troubleshooting** (a 3-bullet section): "I get connection refused" → daemon not running; "tool returns Linux paths I can't open" → known limitation; "permission denied on the named pipe" → file follow-up issue
3. **Smoke test** (the user will do this manually since it requires Windows interaction; document what to test):
   - Manual checklist in the docs page: launch therminal on Windows, configure Claude Code in WSL with the snippet, in a fresh Claude Code session run a tool like `terminal.sessions.list`, verify the response.
   - If you (the agent) can run the bridge end-to-end via WSL during this issue, do so and note the result. If not, mark the smoke test as "manual verification needed" and close anyway — the docs are the deliverable.
4. **No code changes** unless smoke testing reveals a real bug (and even then, file a follow-up rather than expanding scope here).

**Constraints:**
- Docs page only — no code unless there's a bug worth fixing inline.
- Match the tone/format of existing docs in the repo (check `docs/` for style precedents).
- Don't link to non-existent files.
- Path examples should be plausible — use the actual deploy path from build-windows.ps1.

**Verify:**
- `cat docs/integrations/wsl2.md` reads cleanly
- No clippy/test impact since no code changed: `cargo fmt --all && cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings 2>&1 | tail -10`

**When done:** `mcp__beads__close(issue_id="tn-4uko")` with a note about whether smoke test was performed or deferred to manual verification."}

Add MCP resource `terminal://pane/{pane_id}/scrollback` returning historical scrollback above the visible grid. Currently `PaneSnapshot.scrollback` (capped 10k lines) is preserved on attach but never exposed.

**Files:**
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — `PaneSnapshot::scrollback` (the data source)
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/resources.rs` — `list_resources`, `list_resource_templates`, `read_resource`, the URI parser
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/mod.rs` — likely shared parse_pane_uri helper (already exists per the test file you'll find)
- `therminal-daemon/CLAUDE.md` — update the Resources table

**Task:**
1. Extend the URI parser to recognize `terminal://pane/{id}/scrollback`. Reuse `parse_pane_uri` if it can take a "kind" parameter; otherwise add a sibling parser.
2. In `list_resource_templates`, add the scrollback template alongside `content` and `output`.
3. In `list_resources`, add a concrete scrollback resource for each active pane.
4. In `read_resource`, handle the scrollback case: pull `PaneSnapshot.scrollback` (oldest-first Vec<String>), join with newlines, return as text. Support optional `?lines=N` query param to limit to last N lines (default: all). If query parsing isn't easy in the URI handler, ship without it and file a follow-up.
5. Trust gate: Observer tier (same as content). Use `check_resource_access()`.
6. **No subscription support** — scrollback is a static snapshot. Subscribing should return a clear error.
7. Tests: add to `mcp/mod.rs` test module — assert scrollback URI is listed, parses correctly, returns text, and is trust-gated.

**Constraints:**
- Read-only.
- Don't modify the cap (10k lines) — that's a different decision.
- Match the existing pattern for `terminal://pane/{id}/content`.

**Verify:** standard cargo fmt/clippy/test.

**When done:** `mcp__beads__close(issue_id="tn-2q3r")`.
## Retro
- What worked: parse_pane_uri extension + mirroring content arm was straightforward; trust gate is URI-prefix based so no trust.rs changes needed.
- What was unclear: no ?lines=N query param implemented — scrollback is already capped at 10k and full payload is acceptable. Filed as optional follow-up if needed.
- Subscriptions explicitly rejected with scrollback-specific hint."}

Add MCP tool `terminal.workspaces.get_layout(workspace_id)` returning the binary layout tree + focused_pane. Lets TUIs render layout diagrams and reconstruct splits on reconnect.

**Files:**
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/layout/tree.rs` — `LayoutNode` enum (the source-of-truth tree shape)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/workspace.rs` — `Workspace` struct, focused_pane
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — `WorkspaceInfo` (what daemon currently stores; may need to plumb the LayoutNode tree across)
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/tools.rs` — add `handle_get_workspace_layout`
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/mod.rs` — define result types: `LayoutNodeJson` enum (tagged: `{type: "split", direction, ratio, left, right} | {type: "leaf", pane_id}`)
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/trust.rs` — register tool in `tool_category()` as Observer
- `therminal-daemon/CLAUDE.md` — update tool table

**Task:**
1. Define a serializable mirror of LayoutNode in mcp/mod.rs:
   ```rust
   #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, JsonSchema)]
   #[serde(tag = "type", rename_all = "lowercase")]
   enum LayoutNodeJson {
       Split { direction: String, ratio: f32, left: Box<LayoutNodeJson>, right: Box<LayoutNodeJson> },
       Leaf { pane_id: u64 },
       Empty,
   }
   ```
2. Add a conversion from `LayoutNode` → `LayoutNodeJson` (probably in tree.rs as `pub fn to_json_shape(&self) -> LayoutNodeJson`).
3. The tricky part: the LayoutNode lives in therminal-app, NOT therminal-daemon. The daemon's `WorkspaceInfo` only stores flat pane_ids. **Decide:** either (a) plumb LayoutNode into the daemon's workspace state on attach, or (b) move the conversion into a shared crate. Option (a) is smaller — the app already sends layout snapshots on attach (see `LayoutSnapshot` in pane/layout/snapshot.rs). Check if the snapshot already contains enough to reconstruct the tree daemon-side. If not, extend it.
4. Add tool `terminal.workspaces.get_layout` taking `workspace_id`, returning `{layout: LayoutNodeJson, focused_pane: Option<u64>}`.
5. Register in `tool_category()` (Observer), `tool_definitions()`, `call_tool()` dispatch.
6. Tests: round-trip a tree through to_json_shape and assert structure.

**Constraints:**
- Read-only — no mutation tools yet.
- If plumbing from app to daemon is more than ~100 LOC, file a follow-up issue and ship just the daemon-side scaffolding (returning a flat leaf list as a degraded "tree") for this PR.

**Verify:** standard cargo fmt/clippy/test.

**When done:** `mcp__beads__close(issue_id="tn-rgrw")`.
## Retro
- What worked: Existing WorkspaceInfo already has pane_ids + focused_pane so daemon-side shim was small. LayoutNodeJson tagged-union serde worked first try.
- What was unclear: Real LayoutNode tree is not plumbed to daemon — filed tn-vs0u for the full plumbing. Shipped degraded horizontal cascade with `degraded: true` flag.
- Bumped tool count lock-in test 15 -> 16."}

## Why
Multiple features now need user-facing controls that TOML-only config doesn't serve well:

A settings page is also the natural place to surface *that these features exist at all* — the JSONL tailer runs invisibly today, users have no way to know it's happening or to turn it off.

Fix the multi-line hotspot bug: when a long file path or URL wraps across two terminal rows, `hotspot_detection.rs` scans each row independently and the second-line tail (e.g. just `foo.rs`) gets matched as its own hotspot, producing a misleading dotted underline that opens the wrong file.

**Files:**
- `crates/therminal-terminal/src/hotspot_detection.rs` — `detect_hotspots_in_rows`
- Caller in `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` (search for `detect_hotspots_in_rows` or where rows feed into the detector)

**Task — pick option (a) for v1, file follow-up for (b):**
- (a) **Fast mitigation:** When the renderer collects rows for hotspot detection, also pass alacritty's wrap state per row (whether the previous row hard-wrapped into this one). In hotspot_detection, suppress matches that start at col=0 of a continuation row. This loses the legitimate-but-wrapped path but kills the false underline. Document in the test.
- (b) **Real fix (follow-up issue):** Join physical rows that share a logical line into one string before regex scanning, then map matches back to (row, col) ranges across both rows. Filed as follow-up via `bd create` after (a) lands.

**Tests:**
- A row pair where row 1 ends with `crates/therminal-app/src/window/eve` and row 2 starts with `nt_handler.rs:42` — assert the suppression: row 2 does NOT get a standalone match for `nt_handler.rs:42`.
- Negative regression: a normal one-line path still matches.

**Verify:** `./scripts/ci.sh` clean.

**When done:** `bd close tn-4mi0 --reason "..."` and file the follow-up for option (b)."}

{"notes":"## prepared.prompt

Working dir: /home/marci/projects/therminal

Fix the multi-line hotspot bug: when a long file path or URL wraps across two terminal rows, `hotspot_detection.rs` scans each row independently and the second-line tail (e.g. just `foo.rs`) gets matched as its own hotspot, producing a misleading dotted underline that opens the wrong file.

**Files:**
- `crates/therminal-terminal/src/hotspot_detection.rs` — `detect_hotspots_in_rows`
- Caller in `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` (search for `detect_hotspots_in_rows` or where rows feed into the detector)

**Task — pick option (a) for v1, file follow-up for (b):**
- (a) **Fast mitigation:** When the renderer collects rows for hotspot detection, also pass alacritty's wrap state per row (whether the previous row hard-wrapped into this one). In hotspot_detection, suppress matches that start at col=0 of a continuation row. This loses the legitimate-but-wrapped path but kills the false underline. Document in the test.
- (b) **Real fix (follow-up issue):** Join physical rows that share a logical line into one string before regex scanning, then map matches back to (row, col) ranges across both rows. Filed as follow-up via `bd create` after (a) lands.

**Tests:**
- A row pair where row 1 ends with `crates/therminal-app/src/window/eve` and row 2 starts with `nt_handler.rs:42` — assert the suppression: row 2 does NOT get a standalone match for `nt_handler.rs:42`.
- Negative regression: a normal one-line path still matches.

**Verify:** `./scripts/ci.sh` clean.

**When done:** `bd close tn-4mi0 --reason "..."` and file the follow-up for option (b).
## Retro
- What worked: option (a) col-0 suppression is small + targeted; piggy-backed on alacritty WRAPLINE flag already in render cells.
- New API: detect_hotspots_from_text_with_wrap(rows, is_continuation). Old detect_hotspots_from_text kept as a no-wrap wrapper (used by daemon MCP path where snapshot has no wrap state).
- Wire: render.rs computes wrap state from full cell set (not damaged subset) so the previous-row WRAPLINE is always visible.
- What was unclear: pre-existing WIP in working tree (workspace.rs, mcp/mod.rs etc) had unrelated build errors; isolated by stashing other files before running CI.
- Follow-up: tn-a2qd for option (b)."}
